abstract:
Big Data Analytics and Deep Learning represent two of the most pivotal frontiers in modern data science. As both public and private organizations accumulate vast, domain-specific datasets, the ability to extract actionable insights has become essential for addressing critical challenges in fields such as national security, fraud detection, medical informatics, and strategic marketing. Industry leaders like Google and Microsoft increasingly rely on the analysis of massive data volumes to drive business intelligence and shape the trajectory of future technologies.

Deep Learning offers a powerful framework for this task, utilizing hierarchical learning processes to transform raw input into high-level, complex abstractions. By building sophisticated representations from simpler underlying features, these algorithms excel at processing the vast quantities of unsupervised, unlabeled data characteristic of the Big Data era. This paper explores the utility of Deep Learning in tackling core analytical problems, including pattern recognition in massive datasets, semantic indexing, automated data tagging, and accelerated information retrieval. Furthermore, we examine the specialized adaptations required to meet the demands of Big Data, such as managing high-dimensional streaming data, ensuring model scalability, and leveraging distributed computing architectures. We conclude by identifying key directions for future research, posing critical questions regarding data sampling criteria, domain adaptation, semi-supervised learning strategies, and the optimization of data abstractions.
